title: Massage for rehabilitation after total knee arthroplasty: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.

items.ai_reason: Title explicitly identifies the report as a systematic review and meta-analysis.
Background discusses KOA burden, TKA complications, and lack of prior systematic reviews on massage.

PICOS inclusion/exclusion criteria clearly specified, with grouping by outcome and time point.

Two reviewers independently screened records and full texts, with disagreements resolved by discussion or a third reviewer.

Results of subgroup analyses exploring heterogeneity by VAS scale and functional score type are fully reported.
Results of sensitivity and leave-one-out analyses are reported, confirming robustness of the overall estimates.
Funnel plots for all outcomes are reported as symmetrical, indicating no significant risk of bias from missing results.
Certainty of evidence (e.g., GRADE) is not assessed or presented for any outcome.
Results are interpreted and compared with findings from previous systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
Limitations of included evidence (short follow-up, lack of blinding, small sample sizes) are thoroughly discussed.
Limitations of the review process itself (e.g., language restrictions, potential selection bias) are not explicitly discussed.
Implications for clinical practice and recommendations for future research are explicitly discussed.
The review registration status, register name, or number is not provided anywhere in the manuscript.
There is no statement regarding the availability or preparation of a review protocol.
No protocol or registration was reported, so amendments cannot be described.
Funding sources are listed, but the role of the funders or sponsors in the review is not described.
Competing interests are explicitly declared as none.
No statement is provided regarding the public availability of data collection forms, extracted data, or analytic code.
The title and abstract explicitly identify the report as a systematic review and meta-analysis.
The abstract explicitly states the main objective is to assess the effectiveness of US-guided versus landmark CS injections for shoulder pain.
Specifies key inclusion criteria: RCTs comparing US-guided versus landmark CS injections in adult patients with shoulder pain.
Lists databases searched but does not provide the date when each information source was last searched.
The abstract does not mention any methods or tools used to assess the risk of bias in the included studies.
Specifies synthesis methods: pooling data as MD, SMD, or RRs with 95% CIs using R software, plus subgroup analyses.
Reports 18 included RCTs and 1010 patients. Briefly summarizes characteristics as trials comparing US-guided versus landmark injections for shoulder pain.
Presents summary estimates and 95% CIs for main outcomes, clearly indicating that US-guided injection is favored for pain, function, and abduction.
Does not provide a summary of evidence limitations such as risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision among the included studies.

title: Effectiveness of eHealth Self-management Interventions in Patients With Heart Failure: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title and abstract explicitly identify the report as a systematic review.
The introduction outlines the burden of HF, the role of eHealth self-management, and notes inconsistent findings in prior reviews.
The objective is explicitly stated in the abstract and introduction to review the effectiveness of eHealth self-management in HF patients.
Inclusion/exclusion criteria are specified using the PICO framework, and studies are grouped by primary/secondary outcomes and population setting.
Databases searched (PubMed, EMBASE, CENTRAL, CINAHL) and the search date range (up to July 12, 2022) are explicitly stated.
Only combined search terms are provided; the full, database-specific search strategies with filters/limits are not presented.
The use of Rayyan software is mentioned, but the number of reviewers screening records/reports and whether they worked independently is not specified.
Data extraction was performed independently by two reviewers, with disagreements resolved by a third reviewer.
Outcomes are listed and defined, but the paper does not specify whether all compatible results were sought or the method for selecting them.
Variables for data extraction are listed, but no description of assumptions made regarding missing or unclear information is provided.
The RoB 2 tool was used, and assessments were conducted independently by two reviewers with a third for consultation.
Effect measures are specified: odds ratios for dichotomous outcomes and standardized mean differences for continuous outcomes.
The process for deciding which studies were eligible for each specific synthesis is not explicitly described beyond the general inclusion criteria.
While a score conversion for one questionnaire is mentioned, general methods for preparing data or handling missing summary statistics are not described.
The methods section does not describe the specific methods used to tabulate or visually display results, though figures and tables are presented.
Synthesis methods, effect measures, heterogeneity tests (Q, I2), models (fixed/random), and software (RevMan, R) are fully described.
Subgroup analysis based on enrolled population setting (after discharge vs ambulatory clinic) was conducted to explore heterogeneity.
A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was performed to assess the robustness of the pooled results.
Funnel plots and Egger's test were used to assess publication bias (reporting bias) for primary outcomes.
The GRADE approach was used to assess the certainty of evidence for each outcome.
The search and selection process is described with exact numbers at each stage and illustrated with a PRISMA flow diagram.
The paper does not cite or provide reasons for excluding studies that appeared to meet the inclusion criteria.
Each included study is cited, and their characteristics are presented in Table 1 and Multimedia Appendix 1.
Risk of bias assessments for each included study are presented in Figure 2.
Forest plots are provided for all outcomes, presenting effect estimates and confidence intervals for each included study.
The paper does not provide a specific summary of characteristics and risk of bias for the studies contributing to each individual synthesis.
Results for all syntheses include summary estimates, confidence intervals, heterogeneity measures (I2), and direction of effect.
Results of subgroup analyses investigating heterogeneity by population setting are presented in the text and appendix.
Results of the leave-one-out sensitivity analyses are reported, indicating robustness of the findings.
Publication bias assessments (funnel plots, Egger test) for primary outcomes are reported, indicating no significant bias.
GRADE certainty assessments are explicitly reported for each outcome in the results section.
The discussion interprets the findings in the context of existing evidence and clinical implementation challenges.
Limitations of the included evidence, such as heterogeneity, self-report bias, and limited generalizability, are discussed.
The review acknowledges the limitation of restricting the search to English-language literature only.
The review is registered in PROSPERO with the registration number CRD42021246973.
The protocol is accessible via the PROSPERO registry using the provided registration number.
The paper does not mention or describe any amendments made to the registered protocol.
There is no information provided regarding financial or non-financial support or the role of funders.
The authors explicitly declare that they have no competing interests.
The paper does not report the public availability of data extraction forms, raw extracted data, or analytic code.

The title explicitly identifies the report as a systematic review and meta-analysis.
Explicitly states the aim to review evidence for the effectiveness of eHealth self-management in heart failure patients.
Specifies inclusion of RCTs comparing eHealth interventions to usual care in adult heart failure patients.
Lists EMBASE, PubMed, CENTRAL, and CINAHL databases with a search period ending July 12, 2022.
States the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2) was used to assess risk of bias.
Specifies that meta-analyses were conducted using Review Manager and R software.
Reports 24 included RCTs and 9634 participants, with characteristics summarized as adult heart failure patients.
Reports odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals for main outcomes, clearly indicating the direction of effect.
Does not explicitly summarize evidence limitations like risk of bias or inconsistency, only listing GRADE certainty levels.
Concludes that eHealth interventions benefit heart failure patients but emphasizes the need for more high-quality studies.
The abstract does not mention the primary source of funding for the review.
The abstract does not provide a register name or registration number.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of elastic stockings for prevention of thrombosis after orthopedic surgery.

items.ai_reason: The title is not provided or does not explicitly identify the report as a systematic review.
The introduction clearly states that existing study results vary and the preventive effect remains unclear, justifying the review.
The objective to investigate the preventive effect of elastic stockings on DVT after orthopedic surgery is explicitly stated.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria are clearly listed, and studies were grouped by surgery type for subgroup synthesis.
Databases are listed, but the specific date each source was last searched is not provided.
Only general keywords and limits are provided; full, reproducible search strategies for each database are missing.
States two researchers screened studies, but does not specify if they worked independently.
Mentions researchers extracted data, but does not specify the number of reviewers or if they worked independently.
Lists the DVT incidence outcome but does not specify whether all compatible results, measures, or time points were sought.
Lists extracted variables but does not describe any assumptions made about missing or unclear information.
Specifies the Cochrane tool was used, but does not state how many reviewers assessed bias or if they worked independently.
Explicitly states that odds ratios (OR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) were used as effect measures.
Does not describe the specific process used to determine which studies were eligible for each specific synthesis.
Describes converting percentage indices of DVT incidence into actual case numbers for analysis.
States that RevMan software was used to create forest plots and tables were used to display results.
Describes using random/fixed effect models, I2 and Q tests for heterogeneity, and RevMan 5.3.5 software.
Describes using subgroup analysis based on surgery type (ankle vs. hip/knee) to explore heterogeneity.
Describes performing a sensitivity analysis using a case-by-case exclusion method to assess robustness.
States that a funnel plot was used to assess publication bias.
No methods for assessing certainty or confidence in the body of evidence (e.g., GRADE) are described.
Reports 90 records initially screened and 7 included, with a flow diagram (Figure 1) illustrating the process.
Does not cite or provide reasons for excluding studies that appeared to meet the inclusion criteria.
Table 1 cites all 7 included studies and presents their baseline characteristics.
Table 2 presents the risk of bias assessment for each included study across six domains.
Table 1 provides event counts per group, and forest plots present effect estimates with confidence intervals for each study.
Does not provide a brief summary of characteristics and risk of bias specifically for each synthesis conducted.
Presents summary ORs, 95% CIs, I2 statistics, and P-values for the overall and subgroup meta-analyses.
Presents results of the subgroup analysis investigating surgery type as a cause of heterogeneity.
Presents results of the sensitivity analysis showing homogeneity after excluding one study.
Presents funnel plots and discusses asymmetry to assess risk of bias due to missing results for the syntheses.
Does not present any assessment of certainty or confidence in the body of evidence for the outcomes.
Discusses results in the context of previous meta-analyses and explains differences based on patient populations.
Discusses limitations including heterogeneity, publication bias, varying stocking types, and unrecorded intervention durations.
Does not discuss limitations of the review's own processes, such as search restrictions or screening methods.
Concludes that elastic stockings should be combined with anticoagulants and other physical therapies for DVT prevention.
Does not provide any registration information or state that the review was unregistered.
Does not indicate where a protocol can be accessed or state that one was not prepared.
No registration or protocol is mentioned, so amendments are not described.
Explicitly states 'Funding: None' in the acknowledgments section.
Authors declare no conflicts of interest in the footnote section.
Does not report the availability of data collection forms, extracted data, analytic code, or other materials.
Title explicitly identifies the report as a systematic review and meta-analysis.
Clearly states the objective to investigate the preventive effect of elastic stockings on DVT after orthopedic surgery.
Specifies inclusion criteria as RCTs on elastic stockings and DVT published from 2008 onwards.
Lists databases but fails to provide the specific date each database was last searched.
Does not mention any methods or tools used to assess the risk of bias in included studies.
Describes using RevMan software, random/fixed effect models, and subgroup analysis for synthesis.
Reports 7 included studies with 3,116 participants and summarizes surgical subgroups.
Presents main outcome results with odds ratio, 95% confidence interval, and p-values for subgroups.
Fails to discuss any limitations of the included evidence, such as bias or imprecision.
Interprets findings by surgery type and recommends combining stockings with other therapies.
Does not specify any funding source for the review.
Does not provide a registry name or registration number.
